proposed_short: The si + third-person construction (si aggiunge il sale, si vede il duomo) for what one does in general: instructions, recipes, directions, general statements.
proposed_long: The si-impersonal (si impersonale, shading into the closely related si passivante) states what one does or what generally holds, with no particular subject: 'si aggiunge il sale' (you add / one adds the salt), 'da qui si vede il duomo' (from here you can see the cathedral). It is built with si plus the verb in the third person of the plain present (or whatever tense the sense needs). The verb agrees with the thing acted on: singular si aggiunge il sale, but plural si tagliano le patate. English renders it with generic 'you' or the stiffer 'one'; it never means 'it'. It is the classic voice of recipes, directions and notices, alongside the direct tu/voi instruction forms.
rationale: Used throughout the present-usage instructions items (grammar and translation). The glossary has no entry for the construction; the instructions leaf of the present-usage branch is built on it, and later trees (impersonal constructions, passives) will want the same entry. si passivante is folded in as an alias rather than a separate term at this level: the agreement behaviour is presented without the theoretical split.

proposed_short: A verb naming a state rather than an action (sapere, avere, volere, vedere as perception): stative verbs sit in the plain present and resist the progressive.
proposed_long: A stative verb names a state you are in rather than an action you perform: knowing (sapere), having (avere), wanting (volere), and perception verbs like vedere and sentire in their see/hear senses. Because a state has no internal unfolding, stative verbs resist the stare + gerundio progressive: 'stiamo sapendo' is not Italian, and 'sto vedendo il mare' is unnatural where 'vedo il mare' is meant. The same holds in English ('I am knowing' is wrong), which makes statives a safe island where both languages simply use the plain present. The contrast is with dynamic verbs (mangiare, leggere, preparare), which happily take either the plain present or the progressive for happening-now meaning.
rationale: Used in the present-usage explanations for the perception and knowing items (pu_now_02, pu_state_03, trans_pu_it_en_now_02, trans_pu_it_en_state_01). Note: the term 'progressive' used alongside it is already proposed in glossary_suggestions_verb_form.gerundio.json by the GerundioAuthor batch and is deliberately not re-proposed here; if that proposal is ratified, both entries slot together.
